modules.title: Advanced Figma Fundamentals for UI/UX
modules.content:
## Structuring Files for Scalability

Before drawing a single rectangle, you must establish a robust file structure. As an intermediate designer, your goal is to create an environment where anyone—a collaborator, a developer, or your future self—can locate any screen or component without second-guessing. A disorganized file scales poorly, leading to duplicated work and inconsistent products.

Start by separating your Figma files by feature sets or major releases, rather than dumping an entire product into one file. Inside each file, use Pages to divide your workflow logically. A standard page structure includes:

- **Cover & Brief:** Context, goals, and project metadata.
- **Design System:** Where your master components, typography styles, and color variables live.
- **Wireframes:** Low-fidelity structural layouts.
- **UI Screens:** High-fidelity designs.
- **Prototypes:** Interactive flows separated from static screens.

Within these pages, layer naming transforms from a chore into a functional map. Figma’s outline panel uses a slash (`/`) naming convention to create nested groups. Instead of naming a layer "Button," name it `Button / Primary / Default`. Figma automatically organizes this into a collapsible hierarchy. This structure allows you to collapse entire sections of your canvas, keeping the layers panel clean and navigable.

## Mastering Auto Layout for Responsive Design

Auto Layout is Figma’s layout engine. It allows elements to adapt dynamically to their content, resizing themselves based on internal rules. If you are still manually resizing buttons when text changes, you are losing hours to tedious manual labor. More importantly, Auto Layout ensures your designs behave like actual code, responding to content shifts without breaking the visual hierarchy.

To build an effective Auto Layout, think in terms of parent-child relationships. The parent container dictates the rules—direction (horizontal or vertical), padding, and spacing—while the children conform to those rules. 

Consider a standard notification card. If you have an avatar, a title, and a subtitle, you want the text to stack vertically, while the avatar sits horizontally to the left. 

1. Select the title and subtitle, apply Auto Layout (`Shift + A`), and set the direction to Vertical with a gap of `4px`. 
2. Select this text group and the avatar, apply Auto Layout again, and set the direction to Horizontal with a gap of `12px`. 
3. Set the parent container’s padding to `16px` on all sides.

By adjusting the constraints within the Auto Layout settings—such as setting the text to "Fill container" rather than "Hug content"—the card will automatically expand or contract based on the screen width you place it on, just like a CSS flexbox.

## Component Properties and Variants

Components are the building blocks of a scalable system, but combining them with Variants and Component Properties is what elevates a design system from static artboards to an interactive, functional library. Managing dozens of separate components for every state of a button creates friction. Variants solve this by grouping related components into a single set, while Component Properties allow you to toggle specific attributes without duplicating the component.

Component Properties come in four types: Boolean (true/false), Text (string), Instance Swap (swapping nested components), and Variant (changing the component set state). 

Imagine designing a dashboard card for a SaaS analytics tool. This card needs to display different metrics, sometimes with a chart, sometimes without, and occasionally with a call-to-action button. Instead of creating six different component instances, you build one master component.

- Create the base card layout using Auto Layout.
- Add a **Boolean property** named "Show Chart" to toggle the visibility of the graph layer.
- Add a **Text property** named "Metric Value" so you can update the numbers directly from the right sidebar without entering the component.
- Add an **Instance Swap property** named "Icon" to easily switch between a revenue, user, or conversion icon.

This approach drastically reduces the size of your asset library. When a designer pulls this card into their canvas, they configure it entirely via the right sidebar, preventing them from accidentally breaking the component’s internal structure.

## A Realistic Scenario: Building a Dynamic Nav Bar

Let’s apply these fundamentals to a common scenario: building a responsive navigation bar for a web application. 

If you build this with standard groups, resizing the frame will cause elements to overlap or drift apart. Instead, build it using Auto Layout and Component Properties.

1. Create a horizontal Auto Layout for the nav container. Set the space between the logo and the menu items to `Space Between`. This pushes the logo left and the menu right, adapting to any frame width.
2. Make the menu items a nested horizontal Auto Layout.
3. Turn the nav bar into a component. Add a **Boolean property** for "Logged In". When true, it reveals the user avatar and hides the "Login" button. When false, it does the reverse.
4. Add an **Instance Swap property** for the logo, allowing different white-label clients to swap their brand mark into the nav bar instantly.

When you drop this nav bar onto a desktop frame, it stretches to 1440px wide seamlessly. When you drop it on a tablet frame, it compresses to 768px without breaking. The underlying structure is mathematically sound, making the eventual developer handoff straightforward.

## Practice

Create a "Status Badge" component from scratch. 
1. Draw a dot and a text label. Group them in a horizontal Auto Layout with a `4px` gap and `8px` horizontal padding. 
2. Turn it into a component. 
3. Add a **Text property** for the label so you can type "Active," "Pending," or "Offline" from the sidebar.
4. Add an **Instance Swap property** for the dot, allowing you to swap it for a green, yellow, or red dot component. 
5. Save it to your team library.

## Key Takeaways

- **Structure dictates scalability:** Using strict page hierarchies and slash-based layer naming prevents file bloat and keeps design systems navigable.
- **Auto Layout mimics code:** Treating UI elements as parent-child flexbox relationships ensures designs respond dynamically to content and screen size changes.
- **Properties reduce complexity:** Utilizing Component Properties and Variants allows designers to configure multi-state components from the sidebar, eliminating the need for duplicate assets.

modules.title: User Research and Information Architecture
modules.content:
## Translating User Needs into Structured Content

In Chapter 1, we established a rigorous structural foundation inside Figma, treating our files like front-end code. But a perfectly structured Figma file is useless if the product itself is structurally confusing. Before we open a canvas to draw rectangles, we must understand who we are designing for and how the product will logically function. This is where User Research (UX) and Information Architecture (IA) intersect.

For intermediate designers, the challenge is no longer just gathering data; it is translating that data into actionable design decisions. You need to synthesize qualitative research into a logical hierarchy that guides users seamlessly from point A to point B.

## Conducting Competitive Audits and Synthesizing Research

You don’t need to invent a solution from scratch when users are already using existing products to solve their problems. A competitive audit evaluates the strengths and weaknesses of current market solutions to identify opportunities for differentiation. Instead of copying competitors, you are analyzing their patterns to understand user expectations and find gaps.

**Why this matters:** If every competitor relies on a specific navigation pattern, users have likely been conditioned to expect it. Deviating from that pattern requires intentional friction, while adopting it lowers the learning curve for your product.

**How to do it:** Create a Figma frame structured as a matrix. List three to five direct competitors along the top, and key feature categories (e.g., onboarding, core action, payment) down the left side. As you navigate their apps, take screenshots and place them in the corresponding cells. 

Once you have gathered your own user interviews and competitive audits, you must synthesize this data. Raw notes are useless; insights are actionable. Use an affinity mapping exercise in Figma. Create a frame for "Observations" and drop in digital sticky notes (using the Shift + S shortcut) for every distinct piece of user feedback. Group these notes by theme. If multiple users mention frustration with a specific step, that cluster becomes a primary insight: "Users struggle to find the export button."

## Developing User Personas and Mapping Journeys

Data clusters give you insights, but personas give those insights a face. A persona is a composite archetypal user based on behavioral patterns from your research—not a fictional character invented from imagination.

**Why this matters:** Personas create empathy. When you are deep in Figma pushing pixels, a persona reminds you that you are not the user. It forces you to evaluate design decisions against specific goals and technical literacies.

**How to do it:** Build a persona frame in Figma. Include a name, a primary goal, a secondary goal, and a core frustration. Keep it brief. Avoid filler demographics that don't impact design decisions. 

Once your persona is defined, map their journey. A user journey map visualizes the steps a persona takes to achieve a specific goal within your product, highlighting their emotional state and pain points along the way.

**Scenario:** Imagine you are designing a freelance invoicing tool. Your primary persona is "Sarah, a freelance graphic designer who wastes time tracking unpaid invoices." Her journey might look like this:
1. Completes a project (Emotion: Relieved).
2. Opens your app to create an invoice (Emotion: Neutral).
3. Manually enters client payment details (Emotion: Frustrated/Pain point).
4. Sends the invoice (Emotion: Anxious).

By mapping this out in Figma using a horizontal grid, the pain point at step 3 becomes obvious. Your design objective shifts from merely "building an invoice form" to "eliminating manual data entry." This insight directly informs your Information Architecture.

## Structuring Information Architecture with Sitemaps and Task Flows

Information Architecture (IA) is the structural design of your shared information environment. In digital products, IA dictates how content is organized so users can find what they need without friction.

**Why this matters:** If users cannot find a feature, it effectively does not exist. Good IA reduces cognitive load, ensuring users don't have to think about *where* things are. It also aligns your design with how developers will structure the backend routing.

You will rely on two primary IA deliverables: Sitemaps and Task Flows.

### Sitemaps
A sitemap is a high-level visual diagram of the product’s hierarchy. It shows parent-child relationships between pages. 
**How to do it:** In Figma, use the shape tools to create rectangles representing pages. Connect them using the connector tool (press N for the pen tool, or use a plugin like FigJam connector lines). Start with the Homepage/Dashboard at the top. Branch down to primary categories (e.g., Settings, Invoices, Clients), and further down to sub-pages (e.g., Create Invoice, Edit Client). This visual hierarchy ensures you aren't designing isolated screens, but a cohesive ecosystem.

### Task Flows
While a sitemap shows the overall structure, a task flow focuses on a specific user journey. It maps the exact sequence of screens a user passes through to complete a single action.

**How to do it:** Create a horizontal flow in Figma. Use rectangles to represent screens and diamonds to represent decision points. 

Let’s return to Sarah’s pain point: manual data entry. A task flow for "Create Invoice" might look like this:
[Dashboard] -> [New Invoice Screen] -> <Decision: Does client exist?> 
If Yes: [Select from saved clients] -> [Generate Invoice]
If No: [Add new client form] -> [Save client] -> [Generate Invoice]

By mapping this flow before wireframing, you realize you can design the "Add new client" form to automatically trigger when a user types a non-existent client name, effectively bypassing the friction of a separate save step. 

## Practice

Open a fresh Figma file and create a page named "02 - Research & IA". 

1. Choose a simple app concept, such as a local coffee subscription service.
2. Conduct a mini competitive audit by screenshotting the onboarding flows of two existing subscription apps (e.g., Trade Coffee, Blue Bottle). Place them side-by-side and write one sticky note identifying a shared pattern.
3. Create one persona with a specific frustration (e.g., "Doesn't know when the next delivery arrives").
4. Map a task flow for how this persona changes their delivery date. Use rectangles for screens and a diamond for the decision point of whether they are logged in or not. Do not design the UI yet; focus purely on the logical steps.

## Key Takeaways
- **Synthesis drives action:** Competitive audits and raw research are only useful when clustered into actionable insights that highlight specific user pain points.
- **Journey maps expose friction:** Visualizing a persona’s emotional state across a process pinpoints exactly where your design needs to reduce cognitive load.
- **Architecture precedes UI:** Sitemaps and task flows define the logical routing of your product, ensuring that screen designs solve structural problems rather than just aesthetic ones.

modules.title: Low-Fidelity Wireframing and User Flows
modules.content:
With your information architecture established and task flows defined, the next step is translating that structural logic into visible screen layouts. Low-fidelity wireframing is the bridge between abstract routing and tangible interface design. By stripping away visual aesthetics—color, typography, and imagery—you force the focus onto functionality, content priority, and spatial relationships. This ensures that stakeholders and team members evaluate the design based on how it works, rather than how it looks, preventing premature debates about button colors when the underlying navigation might still be flawed.

## Drafting Low-Fidelity Wireframes for Functionality

When you draft a low-fidelity wireframe, your primary goal is to establish a clear visual hierarchy. Every screen has a core purpose, and the layout must guide the user to fulfill that purpose with minimal friction. If everything stands out, nothing stands out. 

To achieve this, rely on grayscale tones, basic geometric shapes, and placeholder text. Use varying shades of gray to indicate depth or emphasis, but avoid introducing colors that might trigger aesthetic biases. A dark gray block can represent a primary call-to-action (CTA) without distracting the viewer with a specific hue. 

**Scenario:** You are designing a mobile app for a local grocery delivery service. Users frequently abandon their carts because they cannot easily see delivery fees before checkout. Your wireframe for the product detail page must prioritize transparency. 

Instead of burying the delivery cost at the bottom of the screen, you place a dedicated information block directly beneath the product image and price. You represent the product image with a simple gray rectangle, the price with a short line of placeholder text, and the delivery fee block with a slightly lighter gray container. Below that, you place a full-width dark gray rectangle to represent the "Add to Cart" button. By arranging these gray blocks, you have instantly communicated the content priority: Image > Price > Delivery Fee > Action, solving the user's primary friction point before a single visual style has been applied.

## Defining User Flows for Primary Tasks and Edge Cases

A user flow is the path a user takes to complete a specific task within your product. While your previous chapter touched on task flows at an architectural level, defining user flows during the wireframing phase requires you to map out every screen interaction, including the detours.

Designing only the "happy path"—where everything goes perfectly—is a common trap. Real users misspell passwords, lose internet connectivity, or encounter empty states. Mapping edge cases during the low-fidelity phase is significantly cheaper and faster than discovering them during high-fidelity prototyping or, worse, after development.

For the grocery app scenario, your primary user flow is: *Search for item → View item → Add to cart → Checkout*. 

However, you must also define the edge case flow: *Search for item → Item out of stock*. How does the user recover? Your wireframe for the out-of-stock state needs to offer an alternative. Instead of just disabling the "Add to Cart" button, your wireframe should introduce a "Notify Me" toggle and a horizontally scrolling block of gray rectangles representing "Similar Items." By wireframing this edge case, you ensure the user is never stuck at a dead end, maintaining their engagement even when the primary task fails.

## Utilizing Figma's Flow Starting Points and Connections

Once you have a set of low-fidelity wireframes, you need to validate how they connect. Figma offers powerful built-in tools for mapping navigation visually, allowing you to simulate the structural flow without building a high-fidelity prototype.

### Setting Flow Starting Points
A flow starting point tells Figma where a specific user journey begins. This is crucial for organizing complex prototypes with multiple entry points, such as a user arriving via a marketing email versus a user opening the app directly.

To set a starting point, select the wireframe frame you want to act as the entry point. In the right-hand Prototype panel, click the dropdown menu next to the frame name and select "Add starting point." A small green "Home" icon will appear next to the frame in your layers panel. You can have multiple starting points in a single Figma file, which is highly useful for testing different onboarding flows side-by-side.

### Connecting Navigation Paths
With your starting points defined, you can link your wireframes to map the navigation. Hover over the right edge of a wireframe frame until you see a small circular node (the prototype connection). Click and drag the node to the frame you want to navigate to next.

In the Prototype panel, define the interaction. For low-fidelity wireframes, keep interactions simple: "On tap" or "On click" navigating to "Navigate to" the destination frame. Use the "Instant" transition for basic structural testing, as animated transitions can distract from evaluating the actual navigation logic.

By assembling these connections, you create an interactive, clickable low-fidelity prototype. You can now run a quick structural usability test. Click through the flow yourself or share the link with a colleague. Does the sequence of screens make sense? Does the user encounter a dead end in the edge cases? Because these are just gray boxes and text, making changes based on this feedback takes minutes, not hours.

## Practice

Open a new Figma file and select the Wireframing template or create a blank mobile frame (390x844). 

1. Draft three low-fidelity wireframes for a "Forgot Password" flow: **Email Entry**, **Check Your Inbox (Success State)**, and **Error: Invalid Email**.
2. Focus purely on content priority. Use gray rectangles for input fields and buttons. Ensure the error state clearly communicates what went wrong and provides a clear path to try again.
3. In the Prototype panel, set the Email Entry frame as a flow starting point.
4. Connect the frames: Tapping "Submit" on the Email Entry frame should navigate to either the Success or Error state. Tapping "Try Again" on the Error frame should navigate back to the Email Entry.
5. Test your connections by clicking the "Play" icon in the top right corner.

## Key Takeaways
- **Functionality precedes aesthetics:** Low-fidelity wireframes use grayscale and basic shapes to enforce decisions based on content priority and spatial hierarchy without the bias of visual design.
- **Edge cases define user retention:** Mapping navigation paths for both primary tasks and error states ensures users never hit a dead end, preventing frustration and drop-off.
- **Figma bridges structure and interaction:** Utilizing flow starting points and basic prototype connections allows you to validate structural logic and navigation paths before investing in high-fidelity UI.

modules.title: Visual Design Principles and Scalable Systems
modules.content:
## From Wireframe to Visual System

In Chapter 3, we mapped out structural logic using grayscale wireframes, ensuring users never hit dead ends and that functionality preceded aesthetics. Now, we transition from structural problem-solving to visual design. A wireframe tells you *what* goes on the screen; visual design dictates *how* it reads, feels, and scales. 

For intermediate designers, the challenge is no longer just making a screen look good. The challenge is making a system that remains cohesive across dozens of screens, multiple user states, and future product updates. This requires applying visual design principles through the lens of scalable systems.

## Establishing Typographic Scales

Typography in UI design is rarely about picking a stylish font; it is about creating a predictable rhythm. A typographic scale ensures that text sizes relate to one another mathematically, bringing order to the interface. Without a scale, designers end up with arbitrary font sizes (14px, 15px, 17px, 18px) that create visual noise and make future maintenance a nightmare.

To build a scalable system, define a base size—typically 16px for body text—and apply a mathematical ratio to generate the rest of the scale. A common ratio is the "Perfect Fourth" (1.333). 

In Figma, you establish this by creating Text Styles. Rather than manually typing sizes, you define styles for Headings, Body, and Captions. 
*   **H1:** 48px (16 * 1.333^3)
*   **H2:** 36px (16 * 1.333^2)
*   **H3:** 24px (16 * 1.333)
*   **Body:** 16px (Base)
*   **Caption:** 12px (16 / 1.333)

By binding these to Figma’s Text Styles, changing your base font family or scaling proportions globally becomes a single-click operation rather than a manual hunt-and-replace mission.

## Defining Color Variables

Color in a digital product carries semantic weight. A red button isn't just red; it signals a destructive action. If that red changes hue slightly from one screen to the next, the trust in the system breaks down. 

Historically, designers used Figma’s local color styles to manage palettes. However, to build truly scalable systems, you must use Figma’s **Variables** (specifically Color Variables). Variables allow you to define raw values and apply them contextually, making your system responsive to themes like light and dark mode.

When building your color variables, structure them in two tiers:
1.  **Primitive Variables:** The raw palette (e.g., `Blue/500`, `Gray/900`). 
2.  **Semantic Variables:** The application of the palette (e.g., `Background/Primary`, `Text/Destructive`, `Border/Subtle`).

By mapping semantic variables to primitive variables, you create a decoupled system. If you decide your primary brand color should shift from blue to purple, you only remap the semantic variable to a new primitive variable, and the entire UI updates instantly.

## Implementing Grid Systems

Grid systems provide the underlying skeletal logic for your visual layout. They enforce alignment, group related elements, and dictate how content adapts across different viewport sizes. 

In UI design, an 8-point grid system is the industry standard. By spacing elements and sizing components in increments of 8 (8, 16, 24, 32, 40), you ensure that layouts scale predictably and align perfectly on standard screen dimensions.

In Figma, you apply this using **Layout Grids** applied to Frames. For desktop dashboards, a 12-column grid with a 16px gutter and 24px margin is highly effective. For mobile, a 4-column grid with an 8px gutter works best. When you design within these constraints, your layouts naturally adapt to responsive breakpoints without breaking visual alignment.

## Visual Hierarchy and Spacing Principles

If typography, color, and grids are your raw materials, visual hierarchy and spacing are the tools you use to assemble them. Hierarchy dictates the order in which the human eye perceives what it sees. Without it, users have to work harder to figure out what to do next, leading to cognitive overload.

You establish hierarchy through contrast: size, color, weight, and spacing. A primary call-to-action button should have a bold background color, high contrast against its surroundings, and ample whitespace. A secondary text link should have less contrast and visual weight. 

Whitespace (or negative space) is not empty space; it is an active design element. It groups related items and separates unrelated ones. This relies on the **Law of Proximity**. 

**Scenario:** You are designing a SaaS billing dashboard. 
*   **Bad spacing:** You place the "Current Balance" text, the dollar amount, and the "Pay Now" button all 8px apart, and you place the "Transaction History" table 8px below the button. The user cannot tell where the balance summary ends and the history begins. 
*   **Good spacing:** You group the balance text, amount, and button with 8px of internal spacing, but wrap them in a container with 24px of padding. You then place the Transaction History table 32px below that container. The increased spacing creates a clear visual boundary, instantly telling the user that these are two distinct sections.

In Figma, you enforce these spacing principles using **Auto Layout**. By setting gap values and padding directly on frames, the spacing scales perfectly as content changes or localizes.

## Building a Localized UI Kit via Atomic Design

A UI kit is the physical manifestation of your design system. However, trying to build a massive, global UI kit from scratch often leads to analysis paralysis. Instead, build a *localized* UI kit—one that contains only the components required for the immediate feature set or product you are designing. This keeps your file lightweight and your workflow agile.

To structure this kit, use the **Atomic Design** methodology. This approach breaks interfaces down into five distinct levels, allowing you to build complex UIs from simple, reusable parts.

1.  **Atoms:** The smallest foundational elements. These cannot be broken down further without losing functionality. Examples: Input fields, buttons, typography styles, color variables.
2.  **Molecules:** Groups of atoms bonded together. Examples: A search bar (input atom + button atom) or a form field (label atom + input atom + error text atom).
3.  **Organisms:** Complex UI sections made of molecules and atoms. Examples: A navigation bar (logo atom + search molecule + profile molecule) or a billing summary card.
4.  **Templates:** Page-level structures that place organisms into a layout grid. They focus on content structure rather than the final content itself.
5.  **Pages:** Specific instances of templates populated with real, localized content and data.

By building your localized UI kit in Figma using this methodology, you create a clear dependency tree. If you update the padding on your primary button atom, every molecule and organism using that button updates automatically. This ensures perfect visual consistency and drastically reduces design debt.

## Practice

Open a new Figma file and create a localized UI kit for a simple "Settings" page. 
1.  Define three Color Variables: `Background/Surface`, `Text/Primary`, and `Action/Primary`.
2.  Create an "Atom" frame and design a basic Toggle Switch component. 
3.  Create a "Molecule" frame and combine a Typography Style (Label), the Toggle Switch atom, and a secondary text style (Helper Text) using Auto Layout with an 8px gap. 
4.  Assemble three of these molecules into an "Organism" frame representing a "Notification Preferences" card. Apply a Layout Grid to the frame to ensure your card aligns to an 8-point baseline.

## Key Takeaways
- **Variables decouple design from intent:** Mapping semantic color variables to primitive palettes ensures global updates and theming require seconds, not hours.
- **Proximity dictates structure:** Applying an 8-point spacing system via Auto Layout creates clear visual boundaries, guiding user attention without relying on heavy borders or dividers.
- **Atomic design enforces scalability:** Building localized UI kits from atoms to organisms creates a strict dependency tree, ensuring that foundational updates cascade flawlessly across the entire product.

modules.title: High-Fidelity Prototyping and Micro-interactions
modules.content:
## Bridging the Gap Between Static and Dynamic

In Chapter 4, we established a scalable visual system using atomic design principles, semantic variables, and Auto Layout. However, static screens only communicate half the story. To truly validate a product's usability, you must simulate how it feels to use. High-fidelity prototyping bridges the gap between a visual mockup and a functional product, allowing stakeholders and test users to experience the flow rather than just view it.

For intermediate designers, this means moving beyond basic screen-to-screen transitions. You need to simulate state changes, dynamic data, and subtle user feedback.

## Wire Complex Interactive Flows

A basic prototype links one frame to another using an "On Click" trigger. While sufficient for linear narratives, modern digital products rarely behave linearly. Complex flows require responding to specific user intents like hovering, dragging, or timing out.

Figma’s prototyping panel offers triggers beyond the click, such as "While Hovering," "Key/Gamepad Input," and "Mouse Enter/Leave." Combining these triggers with Smart Animate allows you to create fluid transitions that mimic native operating system behaviors.

**Why this matters:** Users subconsciously expect interfaces to respond to their physical interactions in specific ways. A button should highlight when hovered on desktop; a carousel should advance when swiped on mobile. When you wire these complex triggers, you reduce cognitive friction and make the prototype feel "real."

**Realistic scenario:** Imagine designing a desktop SaaS dashboard for a project management tool. You have a side navigation bar with collapsible menu items. Instead of navigating to a new frame when a user clicks "Projects," the menu should expand inline to reveal sub-items like "Active" and "Archived." 

To achieve this, you create two states of the navigation component: collapsed and expanded. You apply an "On Click" trigger to the "Projects" text layer, setting the destination to the expanded state. By selecting "Smart Animate" with an "Ease In" transition over 150 milliseconds, Figma automatically interpolates the changes in height and opacity. The result is a smooth accordion effect that accurately reflects the final coded product.

## Implement Conditional Logic and Interactive Components

Historically, Figma prototypes were stateless. If a user toggled a "Dark Mode" switch, navigated away, and returned, the switch would reset to "Light Mode." This made testing complex applications frustrating. Figma variables solve this by introducing state, memory, and conditional logic into your prototypes.

Variables allow you to store data—such as strings, numbers, booleans, and colors—and update them dynamically based on user interaction. By combining variables with Figma’s interactive components, you can create logic-driven flows.

**Why this matters:** Conditional logic allows you to simulate personalization, multi-step forms, and persistent settings. It proves to developers that your design accounts for edge cases, such as what happens when a cart is empty versus when it has items, or how the UI adapts when a user is authenticated versus logged out.

**Realistic scenario:** You are designing an e-commerce app where users can favorite items. You need the "Favorites" count in the navigation bar to update globally whenever a user taps the heart icon on a product card, regardless of what screen they are on.

First, create a number variable named `FavoriteCount` and set it to 0. Bind the text layer in your navigation bar to this variable. Next, create an interactive component for the heart icon with two variants: "Default" and "Favorited." Add an "On Click" interaction to the "Default" variant. Instead of just changing to the "Favorited" variant, use the "Set Variable" action to increase `FavoriteCount` by 1. On the "Favorited" variant, set an "On Click" action to decrease `FavoriteCount` by 1 and change back to "Default." 

Now, as the user taps hearts across different product screens, the navigation bar dynamically updates the total count in real-time, maintaining that state throughout the testing session.

## Design Meaningful Micro-interactions

Micro-interactions are the small, contained moments of animation that occur when a user interacts with the UI. They serve three primary purposes: providing immediate feedback, communicating state changes, and adding a layer of tactile delight.

Effective micro-interactions require restraint. They should be fast—typically between 100 and 300 milliseconds—and unobtrusive. If a user notices the animation more than the task they are trying to accomplish, the micro-interaction is too heavy.

**Why this matters:** Micro-interactions build trust. When a user submits a form and sees a loading spinner transition into a success checkmark, they receive confirmation that their action was processed. Without this feedback, users are left wondering if the system failed, often resulting in duplicate submissions or app abandonment.

**Realistic scenario:** You are designing a "Save Changes" button for a settings page. When clicked, the button needs to show it is processing, and then confirm success.

You build this using component variants and Smart Animate. 
1. **Default State:** A blue button labeled "Save Changes."
2. **Loading State:** The text disappears, replaced by a spinning circular SVG. 
3. **Success State:** A green button with a checkmark icon.

To wire this, apply an "On Click" trigger to the Default state, navigating to the Loading state with a 150ms dissolve. On the Loading state, use a "Wait" action set to 1000ms, followed by a "Navigate to" action pointing to the Success state using Smart Animate. Because the checkmark scales up slightly while the background color shifts from blue to green, the user receives clear, immediate feedback that their settings were saved successfully.

## Practice

Build a persistent "Dark Mode" toggle for a three-screen mobile app prototype. 

1. Create a boolean variable named `isDarkMode`. 
2. Create two color variables: `bg/Canvas/Light` and `bg/Canvas/Dark`. Bind the background of your three main frames to a mode-specific variable tied to `isDarkMode`.
3. Create a toggle switch component with "On" and "Off" variants.
4. Add an "On Click" interaction to the toggle that uses the "Set Variable" action to flip `isDarkMode` from true to false (and vice versa).
5. Test your prototype. When you toggle the switch on screen one, navigate to screen two, and verify that the background color remains in the newly set mode.

## Key Takeaways
- **Triggers define realism:** Moving beyond basic "On Click" transitions to utilize hover, drag, and timed triggers creates prototypes that accurately simulate native platform behaviors.
- **Variables introduce state:** By storing data and applying conditional logic, you can build persistent, stateful prototypes that reflect complex, real-world user journeys.
- **Micro-interactions provide essential feedback:** Restrained, purposeful animations communicate system status and build user trust during critical interactions like form submissions.

modules.title: Usability Testing and Iterative Refinement
modules.content:
## The Bridge Between Intuition and Reality

In the previous chapter, you used advanced triggers, variables, and micro-interactions to build a high-fidelity prototype that feels remarkably close to a real product. However, no matter how polished your visual design is, or how technically sound your conditional logic appears, you are not your user. A prototype built in isolation only proves that the designer can navigate it. Usability testing is the process of stepping back and allowing real users to interact with your creation, exposing the gap between your intended user flow and their actual behavior. Iterative refinement is how you close that gap.

## Planning Moderated Usability Tests

Moderated usability testing involves you, the researcher, guiding a participant through a series of tasks while observing their behavior in real time. Because your audience consists of intermediate designers working with high-fidelity prototypes, your testing should focus on validating specific interactions and micro-interactions rather than basic navigation.

Before opening Figma to share your prototype, you must establish a test plan. A structured plan keeps the session focused and prevents you from leading the participant to the "correct" answer. Your plan needs three core elements:
1. **Specific Objectives:** Identify what you are testing. Are you evaluating the discoverability of a newly designed filter system? Are you testing the error recovery flow when a form submission fails?
2. **Realistic Tasks:** Frame tasks as goals, not instructions. Instead of saying, "Click the calendar icon to pick a date," say, "Schedule a team meeting for next Tuesday at 2 PM." 
3. **Metrics for Success:** Define what a successful task completion looks like. This might be the user reaching the final confirmation screen without assistance, or completing the task within a specific timeframe.

### The Scenario: Testing a Mobile Checkout Flow

Imagine you have designed a new mobile checkout experience for an e-commerce app. In Chapter 5, you built a prototype where users can drag to select a delivery window, and a micro-interaction provides haptic-style visual feedback when the slot is successfully reserved. Your objective is to determine if the drag interaction is intuitive and if users understand that their delivery slot is held for a limited time. 

You will conduct a 45-minute moderated session with five participants. You will share your Figma prototype link via a video conferencing tool, asking them to interact with the prototype on their mobile devices while sharing their screen.

## Conducting the Test Using Figma Prototypes

Figma is uniquely positioned to facilitate moderated usability testing because its high-fidelity prototypes can be shared via a simple link, accessible on any device, without the user needing an account. 

To prepare your prototype for testing, navigate to the "Share" menu in Figma. Under the "Link to prototype" section, ensure your prototype settings are configured correctly. If you are testing a mobile flow, select the specific mobile device frame to prevent the prototype from opening in a desktop browser view, which distorts the scale of your design. 

During the session, your role as a moderator is to observe and probe, not to instruct. 
* **Encourage the "Think Aloud" protocol:** Ask participants to vocalize what they are looking for, what they expect to happen when they tap an element, and how they feel when the interface responds.
* **Resist the urge to help:** When a user hesitates or taps the wrong element, remain silent. The moment you say, "Actually, try swiping up instead," you have invalidated the test. The friction they are experiencing is the exact data you need to collect.
* **Observe the physical interaction:** Watch how they attempt to interact with your variables and micro-interactions. If they tap repeatedly on an element that requires a drag trigger, you have discovered a fundamental mismatch between your design and their mental model.

## Synthesizing Qualitative Feedback

Once you have completed your sessions, you will be left with pages of notes and recordings. The goal of synthesis is to move from raw observations to actionable design insights. You are looking for friction points—moments where the user hesitated, expressed confusion, or failed to complete a task entirely.

Begin by mapping your observations to the specific screens in your Figma file. Create a sticky note for every observed behavior. For example, a sticky note might read: "Participant 3 tapped the 'Reserve Slot' button three times before realizing they needed to drag the timeline."

Next, group these sticky notes by theme. If four out of five participants tried to tap an element that required a drag, you have identified a systemic friction point, not an isolated user error. 

During synthesis, it is crucial to distinguish between subjective preferences and objective usability failures. A participant saying, "I wish the button was blue instead of green," is a subjective preference. A participant failing to complete a purchase because they didn't realize the green button was the primary call to action is an objective usability failure. Prioritize the objective failures first.

## Iterating on High-Fidelity Designs

Synthesis without action is just academic exercise. Iterative refinement is the process of taking your synthesized feedback and directly applying it to your high-fidelity Figma designs. 

Returning to the mobile checkout scenario: your synthesis reveals that users consistently missed the drag interaction required to select a delivery time. Instead of forcing users to learn a new interaction pattern, you must adapt your design to match their existing mental model.

In Figma, you would iterate by adding a visible "Select Time" button beneath the timeline. You would adjust your prototype's interactions, changing the trigger from "Drag" to "On Click," opening a bottom sheet with standard time-picking UI. You might also add a micro-interaction to the original timeline element—a subtle pulse or a tooltip on screen load—to indicate that it is interactive, based on feedback that users didn't realize the timeline was tappable.

This is the core of iterative design: you do not scrap the entire flow. You surgically adjust the components, interactions, and visual hierarchy based on observed behavior, then prepare the design for the next round of validation or final developer handoff.

## Practice

Select a high-fidelity prototype you built in the previous chapter. Before testing, write down the primary task you expect a user to complete. Next, recruit one colleague or friend for a 15-minute moderated session. Share the Figma prototype link with them and ask them to complete the task using the "Think Aloud" method. Do not assist them. 

Take notes on where they hesitate or fail. After the session, make three specific changes to your Figma file based on their behavior. Notice how adjusting a component's auto layout, tweaking a micro-interaction's timing, or changing a trigger type can immediately resolve the friction you observed.

## Key Takeaways
- **Test behavior, not opinions:** Moderated usability testing validates design decisions by observing objective user friction, filtering out subjective aesthetic preferences.
- **Figma bridges the testing gap:** High-fidelity prototypes with advanced triggers and variables can be tested on real devices via a simple link, providing accurate behavioral data without writing code.
- **Iteration is responsive, not destructive:** Effective design refinement involves surgically adjusting interactions and visual hierarchy based on observed user behavior, aligning the interface with the user's mental model.

modules.title: Developer Handoff and Design Documentation
modules.content:
## The Bridge Between Design and Production

Design is only valuable when it translates into a functional product. As an intermediate designer, your prototypes have survived usability testing and iterative refinement, proving they meet user needs. However, developers do not interact with your design through the lens of user experience; they interact with it through the lens of implementation. If your Figma file is ambiguous, the engineering team will either guess at your intent—leading to visual discrepancies and broken interactions—or halt development to ask questions, slowing down the sprint. Preparing designs for production means shifting your mindset from designing for the *user* to designing for the *developer*, ensuring your Figma file serves as an unambiguous blueprint.

## Organizing and Annotating Frames for Optimal Consumption

A disorganized Figma canvas forces developers to spend valuable time hunting for the right screens, deciphering which version is final, and reverse-engineering your layout logic. Organizing your frames is the foundational step of handoff. When a developer opens your file, they should be able to navigate the design as easily as they navigate the codebase.

To achieve this, structure your pages and frames to mirror the engineering build process. Group frames by feature or user flow, and name them using a clear, consistent convention that matches the developers' ticketing system or route names. Instead of leaving a frame named "Screen 3," rename it to `Checkout - Review Order - Default`. 

Beyond naming, visual annotations are critical for communicating intent that isn't immediately obvious. Use Figma’s built-in annotation tool or dedicated annotation components to highlight specific behaviors. For example, if a card component has a maximum width constraint to prevent it from stretching on large desktop screens, draw a quick annotation pointing to the card's edge stating: "Max-width: 600px. Center align within container." This prevents the developer from assuming the card should fluidly stretch to 100% of the viewport.

**Scenario:** You are handing off a complex settings dashboard. The engineering team is building it using a CSS grid. If you simply hand over the final frame, the developer has to guess the column ratios. Instead, you organize the frames under a "Settings Dashboard" page, name the final frame `Settings - Account - Default`, and add an annotation over the main layout: "Two-column grid: Sidebar (fixed 240px) and Main Content (flex-grow)." You have just eliminated a round of clarifying questions.

## Utilizing Figma Dev Mode to Communicate Specs

Figma’s Dev Mode transforms the design file from a visual canvas into an IDE-like environment for engineers. However, Dev Mode is only as accurate as the underlying Figma file. If your layers are unnamed, or your styles are detached, Dev Mode will output confusing, redundant code snippets that engineers will have to manually untangle.

Before turning on Dev Mode, audit your file for cleanliness. Ensure all layers have semantic names (e.g., `Button / Primary` instead of `Rectangle 24`). Verify that all colors, text styles, and effect styles are linked to your published design system libraries. When your file is clean, Dev Mode allows developers to inspect exact spacing, alignment, and CSS/layout code effortlessly.

As a designer, you can proactively use Dev Mode to your advantage. Use the "Ready for Dev" status to explicitly signal which frames are final and locked for the current sprint. This prevents developers from accidentally inspecting outdated exploration frames. 

Furthermore, use the measurement tool (hold `Alt` or `Option` while hovering) to verify your spacing logic. If you notice the Dev Mode output showing `gap: 16px` on a parent container, but you visually intended for the items to be grouped in pairs, you need to fix your auto-layout nesting before handoff. Dev Mode exposes your structural mistakes; fixing them beforehand ensures the developer receives clean, logical layout instructions.

## Documenting Interaction Behaviors, States, and Edge Cases

Pixel-perfect static screens are an illusion. Digital products are interactive, dynamic, and subject to unpredictable data. If you only hand off the "happy path"—the default state with ideal character counts and perfect imagery—you leave the engineering team to guess how the interface should behave when things go wrong. Documenting states and edge cases is where you transition from a pixel-pusher to a systems thinker.

For every interactive element in your design, you must define its various states. At a minimum, document the Default, Hover, Active (Pressed), Focus, Disabled, and Loading states. 

Beyond component states, you must document data edge cases. How does a navigation bar behave when the user has a 40-character first name? What happens to a pricing card if the product title wraps to three lines? What does the dashboard look look like for a brand-new user with zero data? 

Use Figma’s prototyping connections to link these states together visually, but do not rely on prototypes alone for documentation. Engineers need explicit written rules. Create a "States & Behaviors" section adjacent to your main frames. Use a matrix or a list to detail the trigger, the state change, and the expected outcome. 

**Scenario:** You are designing a file upload feature. The default state shows a clean drag-and-drop zone. If you only hand this off, the developer will build exactly that. But what happens when a user uploads a 5GB file? If you haven't documented it, the UI might freeze. To hand this off properly, you design and document three additional frames: `Uploading` (with a progress bar), `Error - File Too Large` (with a red border and error text), and `Success` (with a green checkmark and file thumbnail). You add an annotation specifying: "If file size > 2GB, trigger Error state. Progress bar updates asynchronously." By documenting these edge cases, you ensure the engineer builds a robust feature that handles real-world user behavior without crashing.

## Practice

Select a recent high-fidelity prototype you have built. Choose one specific screen and perform a "Developer Audit." 

1. Rename all layers and frames using a strict, semantic naming convention.
2. Ensure all auto-layout properties are correct and all colors/text are linked to styles.
3. Build a "States" frame next to your main screen. Design the Default, Hover, Disabled, and Error states for the primary button on that screen.
4. Add three annotations to the main screen detailing edge cases (e.g., maximum character lengths, empty states, or loading behaviors). 
5. Turn on Dev Mode and inspect your work to ensure the generated layout code accurately reflects your visual intent.

## Key Takeaways
- **Organization dictates implementation:** Semantic naming conventions, logical page structures, and explicit annotations eliminate guesswork, allowing developers to map Figma frames directly to code components.
- **Dev Mode requires file hygiene:** The accuracy of Figma Dev Mode is entirely dependent on clean layer structures, proper auto-layout nesting, and strict adherence to design system styles.
- **Design for the edges, not just the happy path:** Comprehensive documentation of interaction states, loading sequences, and data edge cases prevents engineering guesswork and ensures a resilient final product.

modules.title: Portfolio Case Study Compilation
modules.content:
## Structuring a Narrative That Highlights Problem-Solving

A portfolio is not a gallery of finished screens; it is a written argument for your competence as a problem solver. Recruiters and design managers care less about the final visual polish and more about the "why" behind your decisions. A compelling case study follows a clear narrative arc: the context, the problem, the process, the solution, and the outcome. 

Intermediate designers often fall into the trap of documenting every single step they took, resulting in a bloated case study. Instead, curate your narrative to highlight the most critical pivot points. Start by defining the business context and the user problem in one or two paragraphs. Then, introduce your specific role and the constraints you faced—such as a tight timeline or legacy technical debt. 

When detailing the process, focus on the obstacles. If you explored three different navigation paradigms before settling on one, explain why the first two failed. This demonstrates analytical thinking and resilience. The goal is to show the recruiter not just what you designed, but how you think.

## Designing Presentation Frames for Final UI and Prototypes

How you present your work is as important as the work itself. Pasting raw Figma frames onto a blank canvas looks unprofessional and fails to communicate the context of the UI. You need to design presentation frames that package your screens effectively.

Start by creating a dedicated "Portfolio Presentation" page within your Figma file. This keeps your working files separate from your presentation assets. Use a consistent background color—usually a soft neutral gray rather than stark white—to make light and dark mode UI stand out without causing eye strain. 

To showcase your UI, use device frames or abstract containers. Figma’s built-in device frames are excellent for mobile app case studies, providing immediate context regarding the platform. For desktop or web dashboards, use abstract browser chrome or rounded-rectangle containers with subtle drop shadows to lift the UI off the background. 

Crucially, you must show the interactive nature of your work. Embed a Figma prototype directly into your case study if you are hosting it on a platform that supports it, or export high-quality GIFs of your micro-interactions. A static image cannot convey the smoothness of a transition or the tactile feedback of a button press. When laying out flows, use numbered badges and connecting lines to guide the viewer’s eye through the user journey, ensuring they understand the sequence of screens without needing to guess.

## Exporting and Packaging Assets for a Professional Portfolio

A polished case study requires high-quality assets. Figma’s export settings give you the control needed to ensure your designs look crisp across any digital portfolio platform, from Notion to Webflow.

When exporting UI frames, always use the PNG format at 2x scale to accommodate high-resolution displays. If your design includes complex shadows or transparent elements, PNG preserves these details without the artifacting sometimes seen in JPEGs. For vector-based icons, logos, or illustrations, export SVGs to ensure they scale infinitely without losing fidelity.

If you are assembling a PDF case study to attach to job applications, file size and image compression are critical. Figma does not compress images natively, so a 20-frame case study exported as a PDF can easily exceed 100MB, which will bounce in email servers or crash applicant tracking systems. Instead, export your presentation frames as individual 2x PNGs, bring them into a layout tool like Adobe InDesign or a presentation tool like Keynote, and export the final document as an optimized PDF. 

For web-based portfolios, package your assets logically. Create a folder structure that separates UI screens, user flows, and prototype GIFs. Name your files semantically (e.g., `01-dashboard-default-state.png`, `02-dashboard-loading-state.png`) so that when you upload them to your content management system, they remain organized and easy to sequence.

## Scenario: Packaging the "FinTrack" Mobile App

Imagine you are compiling a case study for "FinTrack," a budgeting app you designed in the previous chapters. You have just completed the developer handoff, and the engineering team is building the product. Now, you need to translate that Figma file into a portfolio piece.

You create a new page in your FinTrack Figma file called "Case Study Presentation." You set the background to a soft `#F5F5F5` to let the app’s dark mode UI pop. 

Instead of showing all twenty screens you designed, you curate the presentation to show only the core flow: onboarding, dashboard view, and adding a new transaction. You place these three screens inside iPhone 14 Pro frames. To highlight the micro-interaction you built in Chapter 5—where the "Add Transaction" button expands into a full-screen modal—you export a 2x GIF of the prototype and place it directly between the dashboard and modal screens.

For the written narrative, you structure it to emphasize problem-solving. You note that initial usability testing revealed users were overwhelmed by the dashboard's data density. You explain how you solved this by introducing progressive disclosure, hiding advanced analytics behind a tappable summary card. You export the "before" wireframe and the "after" high-fidelity screen as 2x PNGs, place them side-by-side with a brief annotation explaining the change, and package the entire layout into an optimized PDF for your job applications.

## Practice

Select one project you have completed recently. Create a new page in the Figma file called "Portfolio Narrative." On this page, build a three-frame presentation: 
1. The Problem (using a text block and a low-fidelity wireframe).
2. The Process (using a side-by-side comparison of an early iteration and a later iteration, with a one-sentence annotation explaining why you changed it).
3. The Solution (using a device frame showcasing the final high-fidelity UI alongside a GIF of the prototype in action). 
Export the final frame as a 2x PNG and review it on your mobile device to ensure the resolution and text legibility hold up.

## Key Takeaways
- **Narrative dictates perception:** A successful case study frames your design journey as a logical problem-solving process rather than a mere gallery of final screens.
- **Context elevates UI:** Designing dedicated presentation frames with device chrome, consistent backgrounds, and embedded prototypes provides essential visual and functional context.
- **Asset hygiene ensures portability:** Exporting assets at proper scales, using semantic file names, and optimizing file sizes ensures your portfolio looks professional and loads seamlessly across platforms.

# Mastering UI/UX Design with Figma: A Practical Guide to Building Digital Products

## Contents

1. Advanced Figma Fundamentals for UI/UX
2. User Research and Information Architecture
3. Low-Fidelity Wireframing and User Flows
4. Visual Design Principles and Scalable Systems
5. High-Fidelity Prototyping and Micro-interactions
6. Usability Testing and Iterative Refinement
7. Developer Handoff and Design Documentation
8. Portfolio Case Study Compilation


---


# Chapter 1: Advanced Figma Fundamentals for UI/UX

## Structuring Files for Scalability

Before drawing a single rectangle, you must establish a robust file structure. As an intermediate designer, your goal is to create an environment where anyone—a collaborator, a developer, or your future self—can locate any screen or component without second-guessing. A disorganized file scales poorly, leading to duplicated work and inconsistent products.

Start by separating your Figma files by feature sets or major releases, rather than dumping an entire product into one file. Inside each file, use Pages to divide your workflow logically. A standard page structure includes:

- **Cover & Brief:** Context, goals, and project metadata.
- **Design System:** Where your master components, typography styles, and color variables live.
- **Wireframes:** Low-fidelity structural layouts.
- **UI Screens:** High-fidelity designs.
- **Prototypes:** Interactive flows separated from static screens.

Within these pages, layer naming transforms from a chore into a functional map. Figma’s outline panel uses a slash (`/`) naming convention to create nested groups. Instead of naming a layer "Button," name it `Button / Primary / Default`. Figma automatically organizes this into a collapsible hierarchy. This structure allows you to collapse entire sections of your canvas, keeping the layers panel clean and navigable.

## Mastering Auto Layout for Responsive Design

Auto Layout is Figma’s layout engine. It allows elements to adapt dynamically to their content, resizing themselves based on internal rules. If you are still manually resizing buttons when text changes, you are losing hours to tedious manual labor. More importantly, Auto Layout ensures your designs behave like actual code, responding to content shifts without breaking the visual hierarchy.

To build an effective Auto Layout, think in terms of parent-child relationships. The parent container dictates the rules—direction (horizontal or vertical), padding, and spacing—while the children conform to those rules. 

Consider a standard notification card. If you have an avatar, a title, and a subtitle, you want the text to stack vertically, while the avatar sits horizontally to the left. 

1. Select the title and subtitle, apply Auto Layout (`Shift + A`), and set the direction to Vertical with a gap of `4px`. 
2. Select this text group and the avatar, apply Auto Layout again, and set the direction to Horizontal with a gap of `12px`. 
3. Set the parent container’s padding to `16px` on all sides.

By adjusting the constraints within the Auto Layout settings—such as setting the text to "Fill container" rather than "Hug content"—the card will automatically expand or contract based on the screen width you place it on, just like a CSS flexbox.

## Component Properties and Variants

Components are the building blocks of a scalable system, but combining them with Variants and Component Properties is what elevates a design system from static artboards to an interactive, functional library. Managing dozens of separate components for every state of a button creates friction. Variants solve this by grouping related components into a single set, while Component Properties allow you to toggle specific attributes without duplicating the component.

Component Properties come in four types: Boolean (true/false), Text (string), Instance Swap (swapping nested components), and Variant (changing the component set state). 

Imagine designing a dashboard card for a SaaS analytics tool. This card needs to display different metrics, sometimes with a chart, sometimes without, and occasionally with a call-to-action button. Instead of creating six different component instances, you build one master component.

- Create the base card layout using Auto Layout.
- Add a **Boolean property** named "Show Chart" to toggle the visibility of the graph layer.
- Add a **Text property** named "Metric Value" so you can update the numbers directly from the right sidebar without entering the component.
- Add an **Instance Swap property** named "Icon" to easily switch between a revenue, user, or conversion icon.

This approach drastically reduces the size of your asset library. When a designer pulls this card into their canvas, they configure it entirely via the right sidebar, preventing them from accidentally breaking the component’s internal structure.

## A Realistic Scenario: Building a Dynamic Nav Bar

Let’s apply these fundamentals to a common scenario: building a responsive navigation bar for a web application. 

If you build this with standard groups, resizing the frame will cause elements to overlap or drift apart. Instead, build it using Auto Layout and Component Properties.

1. Create a horizontal Auto Layout for the nav container. Set the space between the logo and the menu items to `Space Between`. This pushes the logo left and the menu right, adapting to any frame width.
2. Make the menu items a nested horizontal Auto Layout.
3. Turn the nav bar into a component. Add a **Boolean property** for "Logged In". When true, it reveals the user avatar and hides the "Login" button. When false, it does the reverse.
4. Add an **Instance Swap property** for the logo, allowing different white-label clients to swap their brand mark into the nav bar instantly.

When you drop this nav bar onto a desktop frame, it stretches to 1440px wide seamlessly. When you drop it on a tablet frame, it compresses to 768px without breaking. The underlying structure is mathematically sound, making the eventual developer handoff straightforward.

## Practice

Create a "Status Badge" component from scratch. 
1. Draw a dot and a text label. Group them in a horizontal Auto Layout with a `4px` gap and `8px` horizontal padding. 
2. Turn it into a component. 
3. Add a **Text property** for the label so you can type "Active," "Pending," or "Offline" from the sidebar.
4. Add an **Instance Swap property** for the dot, allowing you to swap it for a green, yellow, or red dot component. 
5. Save it to your team library.

## Key Takeaways

- **Structure dictates scalability:** Using strict page hierarchies and slash-based layer naming prevents file bloat and keeps design systems navigable.
- **Auto Layout mimics code:** Treating UI elements as parent-child flexbox relationships ensures designs respond dynamically to content and screen size changes.
- **Properties reduce complexity:** Utilizing Component Properties and Variants allows designers to configure multi-state components from the sidebar, eliminating the need for duplicate assets.

---

# Chapter 2: User Research and Information Architecture

## Translating User Needs into Structured Content

In Chapter 1, we established a rigorous structural foundation inside Figma, treating our files like front-end code. But a perfectly structured Figma file is useless if the product itself is structurally confusing. Before we open a canvas to draw rectangles, we must understand who we are designing for and how the product will logically function. This is where User Research (UX) and Information Architecture (IA) intersect.

For intermediate designers, the challenge is no longer just gathering data; it is translating that data into actionable design decisions. You need to synthesize qualitative research into a logical hierarchy that guides users seamlessly from point A to point B.

## Conducting Competitive Audits and Synthesizing Research

You don’t need to invent a solution from scratch when users are already using existing products to solve their problems. A competitive audit evaluates the strengths and weaknesses of current market solutions to identify opportunities for differentiation. Instead of copying competitors, you are analyzing their patterns to understand user expectations and find gaps.

**Why this matters:** If every competitor relies on a specific navigation pattern, users have likely been conditioned to expect it. Deviating from that pattern requires intentional friction, while adopting it lowers the learning curve for your product.

**How to do it:** Create a Figma frame structured as a matrix. List three to five direct competitors along the top, and key feature categories (e.g., onboarding, core action, payment) down the left side. As you navigate their apps, take screenshots and place them in the corresponding cells. 

Once you have gathered your own user interviews and competitive audits, you must synthesize this data. Raw notes are useless; insights are actionable. Use an affinity mapping exercise in Figma. Create a frame for "Observations" and drop in digital sticky notes (using the Shift + S shortcut) for every distinct piece of user feedback. Group these notes by theme. If multiple users mention frustration with a specific step, that cluster becomes a primary insight: "Users struggle to find the export button."

## Developing User Personas and Mapping Journeys

Data clusters give you insights, but personas give those insights a face. A persona is a composite archetypal user based on behavioral patterns from your research—not a fictional character invented from imagination.

**Why this matters:** Personas create empathy. When you are deep in Figma pushing pixels, a persona reminds you that you are not the user. It forces you to evaluate design decisions against specific goals and technical literacies.

**How to do it:** Build a persona frame in Figma. Include a name, a primary goal, a secondary goal, and a core frustration. Keep it brief. Avoid filler demographics that don't impact design decisions. 

Once your persona is defined, map their journey. A user journey map visualizes the steps a persona takes to achieve a specific goal within your product, highlighting their emotional state and pain points along the way.

**Scenario:** Imagine you are designing a freelance invoicing tool. Your primary persona is "Sarah, a freelance graphic designer who wastes time tracking unpaid invoices." Her journey might look like this:
1. Completes a project (Emotion: Relieved).
2. Opens your app to create an invoice (Emotion: Neutral).
3. Manually enters client payment details (Emotion: Frustrated/Pain point).
4. Sends the invoice (Emotion: Anxious).

By mapping this out in Figma using a horizontal grid, the pain point at step 3 becomes obvious. Your design objective shifts from merely "building an invoice form" to "eliminating manual data entry." This insight directly informs your Information Architecture.

## Structuring Information Architecture with Sitemaps and Task Flows

Information Architecture (IA) is the structural design of your shared information environment. In digital products, IA dictates how content is organized so users can find what they need without friction.

**Why this matters:** If users cannot find a feature, it effectively does not exist. Good IA reduces cognitive load, ensuring users don't have to think about *where* things are. It also aligns your design with how developers will structure the backend routing.

You will rely on two primary IA deliverables: Sitemaps and Task Flows.

### Sitemaps
A sitemap is a high-level visual diagram of the product’s hierarchy. It shows parent-child relationships between pages. 
**How to do it:** In Figma, use the shape tools to create rectangles representing pages. Connect them using the connector tool (press N for the pen tool, or use a plugin like FigJam connector lines). Start with the Homepage/Dashboard at the top. Branch down to primary categories (e.g., Settings, Invoices, Clients), and further down to sub-pages (e.g., Create Invoice, Edit Client). This visual hierarchy ensures you aren't designing isolated screens, but a cohesive ecosystem.

### Task Flows
While a sitemap shows the overall structure, a task flow focuses on a specific user journey. It maps the exact sequence of screens a user passes through to complete a single action.

**How to do it:** Create a horizontal flow in Figma. Use rectangles to represent screens and diamonds to represent decision points. 

Let’s return to Sarah’s pain point: manual data entry. A task flow for "Create Invoice" might look like this:
[Dashboard] -> [New Invoice Screen] -> <Decision: Does client exist?> 
If Yes: [Select from saved clients] -> [Generate Invoice]
If No: [Add new client form] -> [Save client] -> [Generate Invoice]

By mapping this flow before wireframing, you realize you can design the "Add new client" form to automatically trigger when a user types a non-existent client name, effectively bypassing the friction of a separate save step. 

## Practice

Open a fresh Figma file and create a page named "02 - Research & IA". 

1. Choose a simple app concept, such as a local coffee subscription service.
2. Conduct a mini competitive audit by screenshotting the onboarding flows of two existing subscription apps (e.g., Trade Coffee, Blue Bottle). Place them side-by-side and write one sticky note identifying a shared pattern.
3. Create one persona with a specific frustration (e.g., "Doesn't know when the next delivery arrives").
4. Map a task flow for how this persona changes their delivery date. Use rectangles for screens and a diamond for the decision point of whether they are logged in or not. Do not design the UI yet; focus purely on the logical steps.

## Key Takeaways
- **Synthesis drives action:** Competitive audits and raw research are only useful when clustered into actionable insights that highlight specific user pain points.
- **Journey maps expose friction:** Visualizing a persona’s emotional state across a process pinpoints exactly where your design needs to reduce cognitive load.
- **Architecture precedes UI:** Sitemaps and task flows define the logical routing of your product, ensuring that screen designs solve structural problems rather than just aesthetic ones.

# Chapter 3: Low-Fidelity Wireframing and User Flows

With your information architecture established and task flows defined, the next step is translating that structural logic into visible screen layouts. Low-fidelity wireframing is the bridge between abstract routing and tangible interface design. By stripping away visual aesthetics—color, typography, and imagery—you force the focus onto functionality, content priority, and spatial relationships. This ensures that stakeholders and team members evaluate the design based on how it works, rather than how it looks, preventing premature debates about button colors when the underlying navigation might still be flawed.

## Drafting Low-Fidelity Wireframes for Functionality

When you draft a low-fidelity wireframe, your primary goal is to establish a clear visual hierarchy. Every screen has a core purpose, and the layout must guide the user to fulfill that purpose with minimal friction. If everything stands out, nothing stands out. 

To achieve this, rely on grayscale tones, basic geometric shapes, and placeholder text. Use varying shades of gray to indicate depth or emphasis, but avoid introducing colors that might trigger aesthetic biases. A dark gray block can represent a primary call-to-action (CTA) without distracting the viewer with a specific hue. 

**Scenario:** You are designing a mobile app for a local grocery delivery service. Users frequently abandon their carts because they cannot easily see delivery fees before checkout. Your wireframe for the product detail page must prioritize transparency. 

Instead of burying the delivery cost at the bottom of the screen, you place a dedicated information block directly beneath the product image and price. You represent the product image with a simple gray rectangle, the price with a short line of placeholder text, and the delivery fee block with a slightly lighter gray container. Below that, you place a full-width dark gray rectangle to represent the "Add to Cart" button. By arranging these gray blocks, you have instantly communicated the content priority: Image > Price > Delivery Fee > Action, solving the user's primary friction point before a single visual style has been applied.

## Defining User Flows for Primary Tasks and Edge Cases

A user flow is the path a user takes to complete a specific task within your product. While your previous chapter touched on task flows at an architectural level, defining user flows during the wireframing phase requires you to map out every screen interaction, including the detours.

Designing only the "happy path"—where everything goes perfectly—is a common trap. Real users misspell passwords, lose internet connectivity, or encounter empty states. Mapping edge cases during the low-fidelity phase is significantly cheaper and faster than discovering them during high-fidelity prototyping or, worse, after development.

For the grocery app scenario, your primary user flow is: *Search for item → View item → Add to cart → Checkout*. 

However, you must also define the edge case flow: *Search for item → Item out of stock*. How does the user recover? Your wireframe for the out-of-stock state needs to offer an alternative. Instead of just disabling the "Add to Cart" button, your wireframe should introduce a "Notify Me" toggle and a horizontally scrolling block of gray rectangles representing "Similar Items." By wireframing this edge case, you ensure the user is never stuck at a dead end, maintaining their engagement even when the primary task fails.

## Utilizing Figma's Flow Starting Points and Connections

Once you have a set of low-fidelity wireframes, you need to validate how they connect. Figma offers powerful built-in tools for mapping navigation visually, allowing you to simulate the structural flow without building a high-fidelity prototype.

### Setting Flow Starting Points
A flow starting point tells Figma where a specific user journey begins. This is crucial for organizing complex prototypes with multiple entry points, such as a user arriving via a marketing email versus a user opening the app directly.

To set a starting point, select the wireframe frame you want to act as the entry point. In the right-hand Prototype panel, click the dropdown menu next to the frame name and select "Add starting point." A small green "Home" icon will appear next to the frame in your layers panel. You can have multiple starting points in a single Figma file, which is highly useful for testing different onboarding flows side-by-side.

### Connecting Navigation Paths
With your starting points defined, you can link your wireframes to map the navigation. Hover over the right edge of a wireframe frame until you see a small circular node (the prototype connection). Click and drag the node to the frame you want to navigate to next.

In the Prototype panel, define the interaction. For low-fidelity wireframes, keep interactions simple: "On tap" or "On click" navigating to "Navigate to" the destination frame. Use the "Instant" transition for basic structural testing, as animated transitions can distract from evaluating the actual navigation logic.

By assembling these connections, you create an interactive, clickable low-fidelity prototype. You can now run a quick structural usability test. Click through the flow yourself or share the link with a colleague. Does the sequence of screens make sense? Does the user encounter a dead end in the edge cases? Because these are just gray boxes and text, making changes based on this feedback takes minutes, not hours.

## Practice

Open a new Figma file and select the Wireframing template or create a blank mobile frame (390x844). 

1. Draft three low-fidelity wireframes for a "Forgot Password" flow: **Email Entry**, **Check Your Inbox (Success State)**, and **Error: Invalid Email**.
2. Focus purely on content priority. Use gray rectangles for input fields and buttons. Ensure the error state clearly communicates what went wrong and provides a clear path to try again.
3. In the Prototype panel, set the Email Entry frame as a flow starting point.
4. Connect the frames: Tapping "Submit" on the Email Entry frame should navigate to either the Success or Error state. Tapping "Try Again" on the Error frame should navigate back to the Email Entry.
5. Test your connections by clicking the "Play" icon in the top right corner.

## Key Takeaways
- **Functionality precedes aesthetics:** Low-fidelity wireframes use grayscale and basic shapes to enforce decisions based on content priority and spatial hierarchy without the bias of visual design.
- **Edge cases define user retention:** Mapping navigation paths for both primary tasks and error states ensures users never hit a dead end, preventing frustration and drop-off.
- **Figma bridges structure and interaction:** Utilizing flow starting points and basic prototype connections allows you to validate structural logic and navigation paths before investing in high-fidelity UI.
